title: English Tenses Review
rule: Choose the tense according to time and meaning: habit, action now, past event, completed action, or future plan.
explanation: Bac exercises often test simple present, present continuous, simple past, present perfect, past perfect, and future forms.
examples: Many students revise English every week.
She is preparing for the national exam now.
They visited the library yesterday.
He has already finished the report.
By the time the teacher arrived, we had completed the task.
commonMistakes: Using present perfect with a finished past time: I have visited Rabat yesterday.
Forgetting -s with he, she, it in the simple present.
Using will after when in time clauses: When I will finish is incorrect.

practice.q: My sister usually ___ English after dinner. (study)
practice.explanation: Usually shows a habit, so use simple present with -s.

practice.q: They ___ a school project at the moment. (prepare)
practice.explanation: At the moment shows an action happening now.

practice.q: We ___ the museum last Saturday. (visit)
practice.explanation: Last Saturday is a finished past time.

practice.q: I ___ never ___ such an interesting article. (read)
practice.explanation: Never with life experience usually takes present perfect.

practice.q: By 8 p.m., the students ___ the essay. (finish)
practice.explanation: By a past time can show an earlier completed action, so use past perfect.

title: Gerund and Infinitive
rule: Some verbs are followed by a gerund (-ing), some by to + verb, and some by both.
explanation: Learn common patterns: enjoy doing, avoid doing, decide to do, want to do, would like to do.
examples: Students enjoy working in groups.
She decided to join the English club.
He avoids wasting time on his phone.
I would like to improve my pronunciation.
commonMistakes: I want improving my English.
She enjoys to read stories.
They decided studying abroad.

practice.q: Many teenagers enjoy ___ videos in English. (watch)
practice.explanation: Enjoy is followed by a gerund.

practice.q: He hopes ___ a scholarship. (get)
practice.explanation: Hope is followed by to + verb.

practice.q: We should avoid ___ plastic bags. (use)
practice.explanation: Avoid is followed by a gerund.

practice.q: She agreed ___ the presentation. (prepare)
practice.explanation: Agree is followed by to + verb.

practice.q: They are interested in ___ for an NGO. (volunteer)
practice.explanation: After a preposition, use the gerund.

title: Modals: Present and Past
rule: Use modal + base verb for present meaning. Use modal + have + past participle for past meaning.
explanation: Common modals include can, could, must, should, may, might, have to, and had better.
examples: Students should revise regularly.
You must not cheat in the exam.
She may join the club next week.
He should have apologized earlier.
They must have forgotten the meeting.
commonMistakes: Using to after a modal: must to revise.
Using past simple after should have: should have went.
Confusing must not with do not have to.

practice.q: You ___ respect exam rules. (obligation)
practice.explanation: Must expresses strong obligation.

practice.q: Students ___ use their phones during the exam. (prohibition)
practice.explanation: Must not expresses prohibition.

practice.q: She ___ be at home; the lights are on. (strong deduction)
practice.explanation: Must expresses strong certainty.

practice.q: He failed because he ___ harder. (study)
practice.explanation: Should have + past participle expresses past advice or regret.

practice.q: You ___ bring a dictionary; it is not allowed. (permission)
practice.explanation: Cannot expresses lack of permission.

title: Passive Voice
rule: Passive = be + past participle. Use it when the action is more important than the doer.
explanation: The tense is shown by the verb be. The object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive sentence.
examples: Active: The association planted trees.
Passive: Trees were planted by the association.
English is taught in Moroccan high schools.
The report will be submitted tomorrow.
commonMistakes: Forgetting be: The trees planted by students.
Using the wrong participle: was wrote.
Keeping the active object after passive unnecessarily.

practice.q: They clean the classroom every day. Rewrite in passive.
practice.a: The classroom is cleaned every day.
practice.explanation: Simple present passive uses is/are + past participle.

practice.q: The students organized a campaign. Rewrite in passive.
practice.a: A campaign was organized by the students.
practice.explanation: Simple past passive uses was/were + past participle.

practice.q: People speak English around the world. Rewrite in passive.
practice.a: English is spoken around the world.
practice.explanation: English is singular, so use is spoken.

practice.q: They will build a new library. Rewrite in passive.
practice.a: A new library will be built.
practice.explanation: Future passive uses will be + past participle.

practice.q: Someone has stolen my phone. Rewrite in passive.
practice.explanation: Present perfect passive uses has/have been + past participle.

title: Reported Speech
rule: When reporting past speech, usually move the tense one step back and change pronouns and time expressions.
explanation: Direct speech gives exact words. Reported speech tells what someone said without quotation marks.
examples: Direct: I am tired. Reported: He said that he was tired.
Direct: We visited Rabat. Reported: They said that they had visited Rabat.
Direct: I will help you. Reported: She said that she would help me.
commonMistakes: Not changing pronouns.
Keeping tomorrow instead of the next day when needed.
Using said me instead of told me.

practice.q: She said, I am ready. Report it.
practice.a: She said that she was ready.
practice.explanation: Am changes to was.

practice.q: He said, I have finished my homework. Report it.
practice.a: He said that he had finished his homework.
practice.explanation: Present perfect changes to past perfect.

practice.q: They said, We will volunteer tomorrow. Report it.
practice.a: They said that they would volunteer the next day.
practice.explanation: Will changes to would; tomorrow changes to the next day.

practice.q: The teacher said to me, Revise the lesson. Report it.
practice.a: The teacher told me to revise the lesson.
practice.explanation: Imperatives use told/asked + object + to + verb.

practice.q: She asked, Where do you live? Report it.
practice.explanation: Question word remains; word order becomes statement order.

title: Conditional Sentences
rule: Conditionals connect a condition with a result: if + condition, result.
explanation: Type 0 is general truth, type 1 is real future, type 2 is imaginary present/future, and type 3 is imaginary past.
examples: If you heat water, it boils.
If I revise well, I will pass.
If I had more time, I would read more books.
If she had studied harder, she would have passed.
commonMistakes: Using will in the if-clause of type 1.
Using would in both clauses of type 2.
Forgetting have in type 3.

practice.q: If students ___ regularly, they get better marks. (revise)
practice.explanation: Type 0 uses present + present.

practice.q: If he studies hard, he ___ the exam. (pass)
practice.explanation: Type 1 uses present + will.

practice.q: If I had money, I ___ a laptop. (buy)
practice.explanation: Type 2 uses past simple + would + verb.

practice.q: If she had arrived early, she ___ the bus. (catch)
practice.explanation: Type 3 uses past perfect + would have + past participle.

practice.q: Unless you revise, you ___ ready. (not be)
practice.explanation: Unless means if not; use type 1 for real future.

title: Relative Clauses
rule: Use who for people, which for things, where for places, when for time, and whose for possession.
explanation: Relative clauses add information about a noun. Defining clauses are necessary; non-defining clauses add extra information and use commas.
examples: The student who won the prize is from Agadir.
The app which helps revision is free.
This is the school where I studied.
Amina, who is my classmate, volunteers every weekend.
commonMistakes: Using who for things.
Forgetting commas in non-defining clauses.
Using where for people.

practice.q: This is the teacher ___ helped me. (who/which)
practice.explanation: Teacher is a person.

practice.q: The book ___ I bought is useful. (who/which)
practice.explanation: Book is a thing.

practice.q: Rabat is the city ___ my cousin studies. (where/when)
practice.explanation: City is a place.

practice.q: That is the girl ___ father is a doctor. (whose/who)
practice.explanation: Whose shows possession.

practice.q: 2026 is the year ___ he will take the Bac. (when/where)
practice.explanation: Year refers to time.

title: Linking Words and Connectors
rule: Connectors organize ideas and show relationships such as addition, contrast, cause, effect, example, and conclusion.
explanation: Good writing needs connectors, but each connector has a specific meaning and grammar pattern.
examples: Many students use technology. However, they should avoid addiction.
Education is important because it gives people opportunities.
The city created bike lanes; therefore, pollution decreased.
Although the task was difficult, we completed it.
commonMistakes: Using although and but together.
Using because and so together in the same sentence.
Starting every sentence with the same connector.

practice.q: Choose a connector: The text was long; ___, it was easy to understand. (however/therefore)
practice.explanation: The second idea contrasts with the first.

practice.q: I joined the club ___ I wanted to improve my English. (because/although)
practice.explanation: It gives a reason.

practice.q: She was ill. ___, she went to school. (However/Therefore)
practice.explanation: This shows contrast.

practice.q: The campaign was successful. ___, many students joined it. (As a result/Although)
practice.explanation: This shows effect.

practice.q: Use one connector: ___ he was tired, he finished the essay.
practice.explanation: Although introduces contrast.

title: Phrasal Verbs
rule: A phrasal verb is verb + particle, such as give up, look after, turn on, find out, and take part in.
explanation: The meaning is often different from the main verb alone, so learn common exam phrasal verbs in context.
examples: Do not give up when revision becomes difficult.
Students should look up new words in a dictionary.
Many young people take part in volunteering.
She found out the answer from the text.
commonMistakes: Translating each word separately.
Using a wrong particle: depend in instead of depend on.
Forgetting that some phrasal verbs need an object.

practice.q: Never ___ your dreams. (give up / look up)
practice.explanation: Give up means stop trying.

practice.q: Please ___ the lights. It is dark. (turn on / take off)
practice.explanation: Turn on means start a machine or light.

practice.q: Students should ___ new words. (look up / give in)
practice.explanation: Look up means search for information.

practice.q: She ___ her younger brother after school. (looks after / looks for)
practice.explanation: Look after means take care of.

practice.q: Many students ___ the environmental campaign. (took part in / put off)
practice.explanation: Take part in means participate.

title: Articles
rule: Use a/an for one non-specific singular countable noun, the for a specific noun, and no article for general plural or uncountable nouns.
explanation: Use an before a vowel sound. Use the when the reader knows which thing you mean.
examples: A student asked a question.
An honest person tells the truth.
The teacher corrected the essays.
Education is important for development.
commonMistakes: Using the with general ideas: the education is important.
Using a with plural nouns: a students.
Choosing an by spelling, not sound.

practice.q: She is ___ active member of the club. (a/an/the)
practice.explanation: Active begins with a vowel sound.

practice.q: ___ environment needs protection. (A/The/No article)
practice.explanation: The environment refers to the natural world around us.

practice.q: ___ education helps people succeed. (A/The/No article)
practice.explanation: Education as a general uncountable idea needs no article.

practice.q: I bought ___ book yesterday. ___ book is about technology.
practice.explanation: First mention uses a; second mention uses the.

practice.q: He wants to become ___ engineer. (a/an/the)
practice.explanation: Engineer begins with a vowel sound.

title: Comparatives and Superlatives
rule: Use comparatives to compare two things and superlatives to compare one thing with a group.
explanation: Short adjectives usually take -er and -est. Long adjectives use more and the most.
examples: English is easier when students practice daily.
This exercise is more difficult than the first one.
She is the most active member of the club.
Education is as important as technology.
commonMistakes: Using more with -er: more easier.
Forgetting than after comparatives.
Using the with comparatives unnecessarily.

practice.q: This lesson is ___ than the previous one. (easy)
practice.explanation: Easy changes y to ier.

practice.q: Technology is becoming ___ in education. (important)
practice.explanation: Long adjectives use more.

practice.q: She is ___ student in our group. (active)
practice.explanation: Superlative of a long adjective uses the most.

practice.q: Casablanca is ___ than many Moroccan cities. (large)
practice.explanation: Large takes -r.

practice.q: This is ___ exercise in the test. (difficult)
practice.explanation: Superlative of difficult uses the most.

title: Word Formation
rule: Change the form of a word to fit the sentence: noun, verb, adjective, or adverb.
explanation: Look before and after the gap. Articles often need nouns, be often needs adjectives, and verbs need correct tense.
examples: Education is important. (noun)
Educated people can help society. (adjective)
Students should communicate clearly. (adverb)
Technology has changed communication. (noun)
commonMistakes: Using a verb after an article.
Forgetting plural nouns.
Using an adjective where an adverb is needed.

practice.q: Good ___ helps students understand each other. (communicate)
practice.explanation: Good + noun is needed.

practice.q: She answered the question ___. (clear)
practice.explanation: The verb answered needs an adverb.

practice.q: Morocco needs more ___ projects. (develop)
practice.explanation: Development functions as a noun before projects.

practice.q: The club organized an ___ campaign. (environment)
practice.explanation: The noun campaign needs an adjective.

practice.q: Hard work is the key to ___. (succeed)
practice.explanation: After to as a preposition in this expression, a noun is needed.

title: Prefixes and Suffixes
rule: Prefixes come before a word and often change meaning. Suffixes come after a word and often change word class.
explanation: Common prefixes include un-, in-, im-, re-, and dis-. Common suffixes include -tion, -ment, -ness, -ful, -less, -able, and -ly.
examples: happy -> unhappy
possible -> impossible
develop -> development
care -> careful / careless
quick -> quickly
commonMistakes: Using in- before p or m instead of im-.
Confusing -ful and -less.
Adding a suffix without checking spelling changes.

practice.q: Add a prefix: possible -> ___
practice.explanation: Use im- before p.

practice.q: Add a suffix: educate -> ___
practice.explanation: The noun form is education.

practice.q: Add a prefix: agree -> ___
practice.explanation: Disagree means not agree.

practice.q: Add a suffix: care -> ___ meaning without care
practice.explanation: -less means without.

practice.q: Add a suffix: slow -> ___
practice.explanation: -ly forms an adverb.
